chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and Their Components

questions.question: Define electric current and give examples from daily life.
questions.answer: Electric current is the flow of electric charge through a conductor, usually measured in amperes. Examples include current flowing through a bulb, fan, or mobile charger.

questions.question: Explain the difference between conductors and insulators with examples.
questions.answer: Conductors are materials that allow electric current to flow easily, e.g., copper, aluminum. Insulators resist the flow of current, e.g., rubber, plastic.

questions.question: Draw a simple electric circuit with a battery, bulb, and key and label the components.
questions.answer: A simple circuit consists of a battery connected to a bulb through a key (switch) using connecting wires. Labels: Battery (source), Bulb (load), Key (switch), Wires (conductors).

questions.question: Explain the role of a switch in an electric circuit.
questions.answer: A switch (or key) controls the flow of current in a circuit. When the switch is closed, current flows, and the bulb glows; when open, the circuit is broken and current stops.

questions.question: Define potential difference and its unit.
questions.answer: Potential difference (voltage) is the work done to move a unit charge between two points in a circuit. It is measured in volts (V).

questions.question: Explain what happens when more bulbs are added in series in a circuit.
questions.answer: In a series circuit, adding more bulbs increases the total resistance, causing the current to decrease. Consequently, each bulb glows dimmer.

questions.question: Explain what happens when more bulbs are added in parallel in a circuit.
questions.answer: In a parallel circuit, each bulb receives the same potential difference. Adding more bulbs does not affect the brightness of existing bulbs, as current splits among the paths.

questions.question: Explain the difference between series and parallel circuits with examples.
questions.answer: In a series circuit, components are connected one after another; if one fails, the circuit breaks, e.g., old Christmas lights. In a parallel circuit, components are connected in separate paths; if one fails, others work, e.g., household wiring.

questions.question: Define resistance and its unit.
questions.answer: Resistance is the property of a material that opposes the flow of electric current. It is measured in ohms (Ω).

questions.question: Explain the effect of using a conductor and an insulator in a circuit.
questions.answer: A conductor allows current to flow, completing the circuit, e.g., copper wire. An insulator prevents current flow, breaking the circuit, e.g., rubber cover on wires.

questions.question: Explain why household appliances are connected in parallel circuits.
questions.answer: Household appliances are connected in parallel so that each receives full voltage and can operate independently. If one appliance fails, others continue to work.

questions.question: Draw and explain a simple circuit with a battery, bulb, and resistor in series.
questions.answer: Circuit: Battery connected to bulb and resistor in series through wires. Explanation: Current flows through the resistor first, then the bulb. The resistor reduces the current, controlling the brightness of the bulb.

questions.question: Explain the concept of short circuit and its dangers.
questions.answer: A short circuit occurs when current bypasses the normal path due to a low-resistance path, causing excessive current. It can damage appliances, wires, and may cause fire.

questions.question: Explain the importance of fuses in an electric circuit.
questions.answer: A fuse is a safety device that melts when current exceeds a safe limit, preventing damage to appliances and fire hazards.

questions.question: Define the term 'electric circuit'.
questions.answer: An electric circuit is a closed path through which electric current flows. It consists of a source, load, switch, and connecting wires.

questions.question: Explain why metals are good conductors of electricity.
questions.answer: Metals have free electrons that move easily under an electric field, allowing current to flow efficiently, making them good conductors.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by open and closed circuits.
questions.answer: A closed circuit has a complete path allowing current to flow. An open circuit has a break, preventing current flow.

questions.question: Explain the importance of proper insulation in electrical wiring.
questions.answer: Insulation prevents current leakage, protects against electric shocks, and avoids short circuits, ensuring safe operation of electrical devices.

questions.question: Explain why current decreases when more resistors are added in series.
questions.answer: Adding resistors in series increases total resistance. According to Ohm's law (I = V/R), current decreases when resistance increases for a given voltage.

questions.question: Explain why current does not change when resistors are added in parallel.
questions.answer: In a parallel circuit, total resistance decreases when more resistors are added, but the voltage across each branch remains the same, so current through each branch depends only on its resistance.

questions.question: Explain the working of an electric bell with a diagram.
questions.answer: An electric bell has a battery, electromagnet, hammer, and gong. Current energizes the electromagnet, attracting the hammer to strike the gong. When the hammer moves, the circuit breaks, de-energizing the electromagnet, releasing the hammer and repeating the cycle.

questions.question: Explain the difference between AC and DC currents with examples.
questions.answer: AC (Alternating Current) changes direction periodically, e.g., electricity from mains. DC (Direct Current) flows in one direction, e.g., battery current.

questions.question: Explain the concept of a fuse rating and its importance.
questions.answer: Fuse rating indicates the maximum current the fuse can safely carry. Choosing the correct rating prevents damage to appliances and hazards due to excess current.

questions.question: Explain why electric shocks are dangerous.
questions.answer: Electric shocks can disrupt the heart rhythm, damage nerves, or cause burns. The danger depends on current, voltage, and duration of contact.

questions.question: Explain how proper understanding of circuits and components helps in daily life.
questions.answer: Knowledge of circuits helps in safe usage of electrical appliances, preventing hazards, repairing devices, and efficient energy usage in households and industries.
